summary: McKenzie argues against the prevailing trend of writing list-style blog posts optimized for social media sites like Digg, contending that such content attracts fleeting attention from readers who won't remember your name. He makes the case that investing in quality, in-depth writing builds lasting value through blogroll links (which provide permanent SEO benefit unlike ephemeral social media links), loyal RSS subscribers, and evergreen content that continues attracting readers long after publication. The post reflects his early thinking on content marketing strategy, arguing that building trust through sustained quality writing is the path to monetizing a blog audience. He acknowledges list posts are easier to produce but urges writers to develop genuine craft instead.
key_points: List-style posts optimized for Digg generate traffic from readers who leave in 90 seconds and can't recall your name two minutes later
Blogroll links are the most valuable SEO asset because they never fall to archives, unlike social media links that expire in hours
RSS readers represent a disruptive shift that makes every reader their own aggregator, raising the stakes for consistent quality
Well-written posts have compounding returns because they continue attracting readers long after publication, while list posts require constant production
Building trust through quality writing expands throughout your niche and eventually converts to revenue
money_quotes: If you write for people with the attention span of an ADHD flea who is hopped up on methamphetamines, then this is who you'll end up with for readers.
You don't get blogrolled after the first visit any more than you get to meet the parents after the first date.
You know the one type of link which never falls to the archives? Blogrolling.
RSS readers make every reader into their own aggregator. I cannot overemphasize how fundamental a change this is.
Poor writers ignore the rules, good writers follow the rules, great writers break the rules, and the very best writers make the rules.
If you constantly keep cranking out the top 10 lists to an audience who thinks yesterday was ages ago, you'll have to produce something new every day or the readers will stop coming.
Write in a way that shows off your own voice, personality, and ideas, the better to stand out against a sea of mediocrity.
key_insight: Investing in quality writing over clickbait list posts builds compounding returns through permanent blogroll links, loyal RSS subscribers, and evergreen content that continues working for you long after publication.
